seoTitle: Allegro MicroSystems Technical Support | Design Tools, Resources | LiTong
seoDescription: Access Allegro MicroSystems technical support, design tools, application notes, and engineering resources. FAE assistance and design consultation available.

faqs.question: What technical support does LiTong provide for Allegro products?
faqs.answer: LiTong Electronics provides comprehensive technical support for Allegro MicroSystems products as an authorized distributor: 1) Pre-sales consultation - our certified FAEs help select optimal sensors and drivers for your application; 2) Design review services - schematic and PCB layout review for sensor placement and magnetic design; 3) Magnetic simulation support - assistance with magnetic circuit design for current sensors; 4) Motor control expertise - support for BLDC and stepper motor driver implementation; 5) Reference design access - complete design files for typical applications; 6) Evaluation hardware - demo boards and samples for prototyping; 7) Training - workshops on Allegro products and magnetic sensing technology. Our FAEs have deep expertise in automotive and industrial applications of Allegro's sensing and power solutions. Support is available via phone, email, and on-site visits for complex projects.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for technical consultation, design review, or troubleshooting support on Allegro products.

faqs.question: How can I access Allegro design tools and software?
faqs.answer: Allegro provides various design tools and resources: 1) Application notes - detailed guidance on sensor selection, magnetic design, and motor control; 2) Reference designs - complete schematics and PCB layouts for typical applications; 3) Evaluation software - GUI applications for sensor configuration and data visualization; 4) Magnetic design calculators - tools for magnet selection and placement; 5) SPICE models - for circuit simulation and analysis; 6) Firmware libraries - motor control algorithms and sensor interfaces. All resources are available at allegromicro.com. As an authorized distributor, LiTong provides local support for tool usage and can provide additional application-specific guidance. Contact our FAE team for assistance with magnetic design, motor tuning, or system integration.
faqs.decisionGuide: Download application notes and reference designs from allegromicro.com; contact our FAEs for design tool support.

faqs.question: What reference designs are available from Allegro?
faqs.answer: Allegro provides comprehensive reference designs covering key applications: 1) EV Battery Management - complete BMS architecture with current sensors, contactor control, and thermal management; 2) Motor Control - BLDC, stepper, and brushed DC motor driver designs with control firmware; 3) Current Sensing - various current measurement circuits for different ranges and isolation requirements; 4) Position Sensing - magnetic sensor designs for linear and rotary position measurement; 5) LED Lighting - automotive and industrial LED driver circuits; 6) Power Conversion - current sensing for SMPS and inverter applications. Each reference design includes: detailed application note, schematic and PCB files (Altium, OrCAD), BOM with part numbers, test results and characterization data, and firmware where applicable. Reference designs are available at allegromicro.com/reference-designs. LiTong can provide additional support for customizing reference designs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse reference designs at allegromicro.com; contact our FAEs for customization support and implementation guidance.

faqs.question: How do I get samples and evaluation boards for Allegro products?
faqs.answer: Samples and evaluation boards for Allegro products can be obtained through: 1) LiTong Electronics - as an authorized distributor, we stock popular Allegro evaluation boards and can order samples; 2) Allegro website - samples can be requested directly at allegromicro.com/samples; 3) Evaluation boards - comprehensive selection available with online ordering; 4) Third-party distributors - Mouser, Digi-Key also stock Allegro evaluation boards. For high-volume or production samples, contact LiTong sales for competitive pricing. Evaluation boards typically include: evaluation board with device populated, cables and connectors, software download link, user guide and quick start guide. Most boards connect to PC via USB and include GUI software for device configuration. For custom evaluation needs, LiTong can provide application-specific evaluation setups.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact LiTong for evaluation boards and samples; visit allegromicro.com for direct sample requests.

faqs.question: What documentation is available for Allegro products?
faqs.answer: Allegro provides comprehensive documentation: 1) Datasheets - complete electrical specifications, magnetic characteristics, and package information; 2) Application notes - detailed guidance on sensor applications, magnetic design, and motor control; 3) Technical articles - in-depth discussions of Hall-effect technology and design considerations; 4) User guides - setup and operation instructions for evaluation boards; 5) Safety manuals - ISO 26262 functional safety documentation; 6) Reliability data - FIT rates and qualification reports. Documentation is available at allegromicro.com in PDF format. Key resources include application notes for current sensor design, magnetic sensor integration, and motor driver implementation. LiTong maintains a library of Allegro documentation and can provide additional application-specific guidance. For automotive applications, functional safety documentation is available upon request.
faqs.decisionGuide: Access datasheets and app notes at allegromicro.com; contact LiTong for additional application-specific documentation.

faqs.question: How do I design magnetic circuits for Allegro sensors?
faqs.answer: Designing magnetic circuits for Allegro sensors involves: 1) Magnet selection - choose appropriate material (NdFeB for high field, ferrite for cost-sensitive), size, and grade; 2) Air gap design - Hall sensors require magnetic field in range of 100-1000 Gauss; calculate gap based on magnet strength; 3) Target design - ferromagnetic target affects field strength and shape; optimize for linear response; 4) Mechanical alignment - ensure proper sensor-to-magnet positioning for consistent readings; 5) Temperature compensation - consider magnet temperature coefficient in design. Tools available: Allegro's magnetic design calculators, finite element analysis (FEA) software, application notes with design examples. Key considerations: Magnetic field strength at sensor location, field uniformity across travel range, temperature effects on magnet properties, and mechanical tolerances. Contact LiTong FAEs for magnetic design review and optimization.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Allegro magnetic calculators; target 100-1000 Gauss field strength; consider temperature effects; contact FAEs for design review.

faqs.question: What is the typical lead time for Allegro products?
faqs.answer: Allegro product lead times vary by product type and market conditions: Standard products (high volume): 8-12 weeks typical; Automotive qualified devices: 12-16 weeks due to additional testing; New products: May have longer lead times during ramp-up; Custom variants: 16-20 weeks for special configurations. Lead time reduction strategies: 1) Forecast sharing - provide demand forecasts for better planning; 2) Safety stock - maintain buffer inventory for critical components; 3) Alternative parts - identify second sources where possible; 4) Long-term agreements - secure supply with LTAs for high-volume programs. LiTong Electronics maintains inventory of popular Allegro products for immediate availability. Contact our sales team for current lead times and supply planning assistance. For automotive programs, Allegro offers 15+ year product lifecycle commitment.
faqs.decisionGuide: Plan for 8-16 week lead times; use LiTong inventory for immediate needs; contact sales for supply planning.

faqs.question: Does Allegro provide functional safety documentation?
faqs.answer: Yes, Allegro provides functional safety documentation for automotive applications: 1) FMEDA (Failure Modes Effects and Diagnostics Analysis) - detailed analysis of failure modes and diagnostic coverage; 2) Safety manuals - guidelines for integrating devices into safety-critical systems; 3) ISO 26262 compliance - many products developed per ASIL requirements; 4) PPAP documentation - Production Part Approval Process for automotive qualification; 5) AEC-Q100 reports - qualification test results. Available safety features: Built-in self-test (BIST), diagnostic outputs, redundant sensing elements, fault detection circuits. To access safety documentation: Contact Allegro or LiTong with specific device part numbers; Sign NDA for detailed safety analysis; Provide information on target ASIL level and application. Safety documentation is typically provided for devices used in steering, braking, and battery management systems.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact LiTong for FMEDA and safety manuals; provide ASIL requirements and application details.

articles.title: How to Select the Right Current Sensor for Your Application
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide to selecting Hall-effect current sensors based on current range, isolation, bandwidth, and application requirements.
content: Selecting the right current sensor is critical for system performance and safety. This guide walks through the key selection criteria for Allegro Hall-effect current sensors.
Allegro offers several current sensor architectures:
Isolated sensors provide galvanic isolation between current path and sensor circuitry:
- Integrated current conductor and Hall sensor in one package
- Isolation voltage up to 4.8kV for high-voltage applications
- Ideal for EV battery management, motor drives, and power supplies
Select sensor with rated range 1.5-2× maximum expected current:
- Too small: sensor saturates, loses accuracy
- Too large: reduced resolution at low currents

faeInsights.insight: In my 12 years supporting current sensor applications, I've seen many designs fail due to inadequate attention to magnetic interference and thermal design. The biggest mistake is ignoring external magnetic fields - in motor drives and power converters, adjacent conductors can create fields of 100-500 Gauss, causing significant errors. Always use differential sensors (ACS37610) in high-field environments. Another common issue is thermal design - high-current sensors dissipate significant power (P = I²R). At 200A with 0.1mΩ, that's 4W. Without proper heatsinking, temperature rise can exceed 50°C, causing drift. Use busbar mounting or adequate copper area for heat dissipation.
faeInsights.logic: Current sensor selection follows systematic approach: 1) Define current range and direction (uni/bidirectional); 2) Determine isolation requirements from working voltage; 3) Select bandwidth based on signal frequency; 4) Choose accuracy based on application needs; 5) Consider package and mounting for thermal management; 6) Evaluate magnetic environment for interference susceptibility.
keyTakeaways: Use isolated sensors for high-voltage (> 60V) applications
Select current range 1.5-2× maximum expected current
Consider bandwidth needs: 50-100kHz for motor control, 1MHz for fast protection
Design for thermal management at maximum current
Use differential sensors in high magnetic field environments
bestPractices: Always include 50% margin in current range selection
Use differential sensors in motor drive applications
Implement RC filtering at sensor output
Calibrate offset at system level
Verify thermal performance at max current
troubleshootingTips: Check for magnetic interference with gaussmeter
Verify thermal performance with IR camera
Measure actual vs expected sensitivity
Check for proper decoupling on supply pins
Validate isolation integrity with hipot tester

customerCases.problem: Existing shunt sensors suffered from poor isolation and thermal drift
customerCases.diagnosis: Discrete shunt + amplifier solution had isolation limitations and temperature sensitivity
customerCases.solution: Redesigned with ACS720 isolated sensors with integrated temperature compensation
customerCases.results: Improved accuracy from ±3% to ±1%, eliminated external isolation components

faqs.question: What is the difference between Hall-effect and shunt current sensing?
faqs.answer: Hall-effect and shunt sensing are two different current measurement approaches: Hall-effect sensors measure the magnetic field generated by current flow, providing inherent galvanic isolation. Advantages: no insertion loss (low resistance < 1mΩ), isolation up to 4.8kV, bidirectional measurement, fast response. Disadvantages: higher cost, magnetic sensitivity, temperature drift. Shunt resistors measure voltage drop across known resistance. Advantages: high accuracy, low cost, DC-DC measurement. Disadvantages: power loss (I²R), no isolation, requires amplifier. For high-voltage applications, Hall-effect is preferred for safety. For precision low-voltage measurement, shunts may be better. Allegro's integrated Hall sensors combine the best of both: isolation with minimal power loss.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Hall-effect for high-voltage and isolated applications; use shunts for precision low-voltage measurement.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the required current sensor range?
faqs.answer: Current sensor range selection involves: 1) Determine maximum expected current including transients and fault conditions; 2) Add 50% margin for headroom: Sensor Range = 1.5 × Imax_normal; 3) Consider crest factor for AC applications - peak current may be 2-3× RMS; 4) Verify minimum current can be measured with adequate resolution. Example: Motor drive with 10A RMS normal current, 15A peak: Minimum range = 1.5 × 15A = 22.5A; Select ±30A sensor (ACS712-30A). Resolution check: With 66mV/A sensitivity and 12-bit ADC (5V/4096 = 1.22mV LSB): Current resolution = 1.22mV / 66mV/A = 18.5mA per LSB. At 1A, this is 1.85% resolution, adequate for most applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select range = 1.5 × Imax; verify resolution at minimum current; consider crest factor for AC.

faqs.question: What affects current sensor accuracy?
faqs.answer: Current sensor accuracy is affected by multiple factors: 1) Temperature drift - sensitivity and offset change with temperature; 2) External magnetic fields - nearby conductors and magnets create interference; 3) Power supply variation - ratiometric outputs track Vcc, but noise affects accuracy; 4) Mechanical stress - package stress affects Hall element; 5) Aging - long-term drift over product lifetime. Allegro specifications: Offset drift: ±5-15mV over temperature; Sensitivity drift: ±0.5-1.5%; Total error: ±1-5% depending on device. Improvement methods: Temperature compensation using integrated temp sensor; Shielding or differential sensing for magnetic interference; Stable power supply with adequate decoupling; Calibration at operating temperature.
faqs.decisionGuide: Expect ±1-5% total error; use temperature compensation; shield from magnetic fields; calibrate for best accuracy.

faqs.question: How do I protect current sensors from overcurrent?
faqs.answer: Current sensor protection strategies: 1) Select sensor with adequate current range - sustained overcurrent causes heating and damage; 2) Implement fast overcurrent detection - use OCD output or external comparator; 3) Add series resistance - limits fault current (trade-off with accuracy); 4) Use fuses or circuit breakers - protect wiring and sensor; 5) Thermal management - ensure adequate heat dissipation. Allegro sensors include: Overcurrent detection (OCD) output with < 1μs response; Internal current limiting; Thermal shutdown protection. Protection design: Set OCD threshold at 1.2-1.5× normal max current; Connect OCD to gate driver shutdown or MCU interrupt; Implement software protection as backup (slower but redundant). For extreme fault conditions, external fuses provide ultimate protection.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use OCD feature for fast protection; set threshold at 1.2-1.5× Imax; implement redundant software protection.

faqs.question: Can I use current sensors in high magnetic field environments?
faqs.answer: Yes, but careful design is needed for high magnetic field environments: Sources of interference: Adjacent current-carrying conductors (100-1000 Gauss), transformers and inductors, motors and actuators, external magnets. Mitigation strategies: 1) Use differential sensors (ACS37610) - common-mode rejection > 40dB; 2) Physical separation - maximize distance from field sources; 3) Magnetic shielding - use mu-metal or ferrite shields; 4) Orientation - align sensor to minimize field coupling; 5) Filtering - remove interference in frequency domain. Test method: Measure sensor output with no current (should be zero); Apply known current and verify accuracy; Check for drift when nearby circuits operate. Differential sensors are essential for motor drives and power converters where fields of 500+ Gauss are common.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use differential sensors for > 100 Gauss environments; implement shielding; maximize separation from field sources.

articles.title: Magnetic Sensor Design Guide for Position Sensing
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide to designing magnetic circuits for Hall-effect position sensors including magnet selection, air gap optimization, and mechanical alignment.
content: Magnetic position sensing requires careful design of the magnetic circuit to achieve accurate and reliable measurements. This guide covers the fundamentals of magnetic sensor design using Allegro Hall-effect sensors.
A magnetic position sensing system consists of:
- Grade: Determines field strength and temperature stability
- Shape: Disk, ring, or block depending on application
- Affects field strength at sensor location
- Hall-effect sensor measures magnetic flux density
- Calculate required field strength (100-1000 Gauss typical)
- Select material based on temperature requirements
- Determine size based on air gap and field uniformity
- Verify field strength across operating range
- Position sensor for optimal field detection

faeInsights.insight: In my 10 years designing magnetic position sensors, the most common issue is inadequate field strength at the sensor location. Many designers underestimate how quickly magnetic field drops with distance. The field strength decreases with the cube of distance for point sources, so a small increase in air gap dramatically reduces field strength. I always recommend using magnetic simulation software (like ANSYS Maxwell or Allegro's own tools) to verify the design before building prototypes. Another common mistake is ignoring temperature effects - NdFeB magnets lose about 0.1% field strength per °C, which can cause significant drift over the automotive temperature range.
faeInsights.logic: Magnetic sensor design follows systematic approach: 1) Define measurement requirements (range, accuracy, environment); 2) Select appropriate magnet material and size; 3) Design air gap for optimal field strength; 4) Position sensor for maximum sensitivity; 5) Verify with simulation and prototype testing; 6) Implement calibration for production.
keyTakeaways: Use magnetic simulation to verify field strength before prototyping
Field strength decreases rapidly with air gap distance
Consider temperature effects on magnet properties
Target 100-1000 Gauss field strength at sensor
Implement calibration for production accuracy
commonPitfalls: Inadequate field strength due to large air gap
Poor mechanical stability causing position drift
bestPractices: Use simulation tools for initial design verification
Include temperature compensation in firmware
Design for worst-case mechanical tolerances
Implement shielding for noisy environments
Calibrate each unit for production accuracy
troubleshootingTips: Measure actual field strength with gaussmeter
Check for external field interference
Verify mechanical stability under vibration
Test over full temperature range
Compare actual vs simulated field distribution

customerCases.problem: Position sensor drifted with temperature causing engine performance issues
customerCases.diagnosis: Analysis showed magnet temperature coefficient caused 15% field variation over temperature range
customerCases.solution: Redesigned with temperature-compensated magnet and implemented software calibration
customerCases.results: Reduced position error from ±5% to ±1% over full temperature range

faqs.question: What magnet material should I use for my application?
faqs.answer: Magnet material selection depends on your requirements: NdFeB (Neodymium): Highest field strength (1.0-1.4 Tesla), best for small air gaps and high resolution, temperature coefficient -0.1%/°C, higher cost. Use for: high-precision applications, small packages, automotive. Ferrite: Lower field strength (0.3-0.4 Tesla), cost-effective, temperature coefficient -0.2%/°C. Use for: cost-sensitive applications, large air gaps, consumer electronics. SmCo (Samarium Cobalt): High field strength, excellent temperature stability (-0.03%/°C), expensive. Use for: high-temperature applications, aerospace. For most automotive position sensing, NdFeB N35-N52 grades provide the best balance of performance and cost.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use NdFeB for high performance; Ferrite for cost-sensitive apps; SmCo for extreme temperatures.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the required air gap?
faqs.answer: Air gap calculation involves balancing field strength and mechanical constraints: Target field strength at sensor: 100-1000 Gauss (0.01-0.1 Tesla). For a cylindrical magnet, field at distance z: B(z) = (Br/2) × [(L+z)/√(R²+(L+z)²) - z/√(R²+z²)], where Br is remanence, L is length, R is radius. Example: NdFeB N42 (Br=1.3T), 10mm diameter, 5mm thick, target 500 Gauss: Solve for z ≈ 3-4mm air gap. Practical considerations: Smaller gap = stronger field but tighter mechanical tolerances; Larger gap = weaker field but easier assembly; Always verify with simulation or measurement. Use Allegro's magnetic design calculators for quick estimation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Target 100-1000 Gauss at sensor; use simulation tools; balance field strength vs mechanical tolerance.

articles.title: Motor Control Fundamentals with Allegro Drivers
articles.summary: Essential guide to motor control using Allegro motor drivers covering BLDC, stepper, and brushed DC motor control techniques.
content: Motor control is a critical application for Allegro's power IC portfolio. This guide covers the fundamentals of controlling different motor types using Allegro drivers.
- Move in discrete steps (typically 1.8° per step)
- AMT49105: Integrated driver with sensorless control
- Features: Sinusoidal commutation, speed control, protection
- A4988: Simple stepper driver with translator
- Features: Microstepping, current regulation, decay modes
- A4950: Full-bridge driver with current sense
- Features: PWM control, current limiting, thermal protection

faeInsights.insight: In my 14 years designing motor control systems, I've learned that successful designs start with understanding the motor characteristics and load requirements. The biggest mistake is undersizing the driver - always include at least 50% current margin above the motor's rated current to handle startup and overload conditions. For BLDC motors, sensorless control works well for most applications, but if you need precise position control at low speeds, Hall sensors are essential. Thermal management is critical - motor drivers can dissipate significant power. Always calculate power dissipation and ensure adequate heatsinking. For automotive applications, EMI compliance is crucial - proper PCB layout with attention to current loops and grounding is essential.
faeInsights.logic: Motor control design follows systematic approach: 1) Define motor type and specifications; 2) Calculate power requirements and select driver; 3) Choose control method (sensorless, sensored, open/closed loop); 4) Design power stage and protection; 5) Implement control algorithm; 6) Test under all operating conditions including fault scenarios.
keyTakeaways: Include 50% current margin for motor driver selection
Sensorless control works for most BLDC applications
Thermal management critical for reliability
EMI compliance requires careful PCB layout
Test under all operating and fault conditions
commonPitfalls: Poor PCB layout causing EMI failures
bestPractices: Use Allegro reference designs as starting point
Implement comprehensive fault protection
Add current sensing for torque control
Use proper gate drive for fast switching
Include EMI filtering on power and signal lines
troubleshootingTips: Check motor phase currents with current probe
Verify gate drive waveforms with oscilloscope
Measure temperature rise under load
Check for audible noise indicating resonance
Verify protection circuits trip at correct levels

customerCases.problem: Existing motor drivers overheated and failed under high ambient temperatures
customerCases.diagnosis: Analysis showed insufficient thermal design and lack of overload protection
customerCases.solution: Redesigned with AMT49105 drivers and improved thermal management
customerCases.results: Eliminated thermal failures, improved efficiency by 15%, passed 85°C ambient testing

faqs.question: What is the difference between sensored and sensorless BLDC control?
faqs.answer: Sensored BLDC control uses Hall sensors to detect rotor position: Advantages - reliable startup, precise position control, works at low speeds. Disadvantages - additional cost, wiring complexity, sensor reliability concerns. Use for: precise positioning, low-speed operation, high-reliability applications. Sensorless BLDC control uses back-EMF sensing: Advantages - lower cost, simpler wiring, higher reliability. Disadvantages - difficult startup (unknown position), poor low-speed performance, complex algorithms. Use for: fans, pumps, compressors where precise position not needed. Allegro's AMT49105 supports both modes - use sensored for difficult loads, sensorless for cost-sensitive applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use sensored for precise position control; sensorless for cost-sensitive fan/pump applications; AMT49105 supports both.

faqs.question: How do I calculate power dissipation in motor drivers?
faqs.answer: Motor driver power dissipation includes conduction and switching losses: Conduction loss: P_cond = I² × Rds(on) × duty cycle. For MOSFET with 10mΩ Rds(on) at 10A: P = 100 × 0.01 = 1W per switch. Switching loss: P_sw = 0.5 × V × I × (tr + tf) × fsw, where tr/tf are rise/fall times, fsw is switching frequency. Example: 50V, 10A, tr+tf=100ns, 20kHz: P_sw = 0.5 × 50 × 10 × 100e-9 × 20000 = 0.5W. Total dissipation: Sum of all switch losses. For three-phase bridge: 6 switches × (1W + 0.5W) = 9W total. Thermal design: Calculate junction temperature Tj = Ta + P × θja. Ensure Tj < 150°C. Use heatsinks or copper area for thermal management.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate conduction and switching losses; ensure Tj < 150°C; use adequate heatsinking for high-power applications.

articles.title: EV Battery Management System Design Guide
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide to designing battery management systems for electric vehicles using Allegro current sensors and magnetic position sensors.
content: Electric vehicle battery management systems require precise current sensing, high-voltage isolation, and functional safety. This guide covers BMS design using Allegro sensing solutions.
- Features: Differential sensing, OCD output, AEC-Q100
- A1335: Angle sensor for contactor position
- Features: Non-contact, high reliability, automotive qualified

faeInsights.insight: In my 11 years working on automotive BMS designs, the current sensor is one of the most critical components. For 800V EV systems, the 4.8kV isolation of the ACS37610 provides essential safety margin. I've seen designs fail because they used lower isolation rated sensors that couldn't withstand the working voltage long-term. The differential sensing is crucial in EVs - the magnetic fields from motor cables and inverters can cause significant errors in single-element sensors. Always implement both the analog output for continuous monitoring and the OCD output for fast protection. The OCD can directly shut down gate drivers in < 1μs, which is essential for protecting SiC MOSFETs that can fail in microseconds during short circuits.
faeInsights.logic: EV BMS design priorities: 1) Safety - high isolation voltage essential for HV systems; 2) Protection - fast fault detection prevents battery damage; 3) Accuracy - precise current for SOC/SOH estimation; 4) Reliability - automotive qualification required for 15-year life. Allegro's integrated sensors address all priorities with single-device solutions.
keyTakeaways: Use 4.8kV isolation sensors for 800V EV systems
Differential sensing essential for noisy EV environment
Implement both analog monitoring and OCD protection
Fast OCD (< 1μs) protects SiC devices during faults
Automotive qualification ensures long-term reliability
commonPitfalls: Insufficient isolation rating for working voltage
Slow protection response allowing device damage
Inadequate thermal design for high current
bestPractices: Use busbar mounting for best thermal performance
Implement redundant protection (hardware + software)
Route differential traces as twisted pair
Add RC filtering at ADC input for noise reduction
Validate isolation with hipot testing
troubleshootingTips: Check thermal performance with IR camera at max current
Verify isolation integrity with hipot tester
Measure OCD response time with oscilloscope
Check for external field interference with gaussmeter
Validate SOC accuracy with Coulomb counting test

customerCases.problem: Existing current sensing solution had insufficient isolation for 800V system
customerCases.diagnosis: Analysis showed 2.1kV isolation inadequate for 800V working voltage long-term reliability
customerCases.solution: Redesigned with ACS37610 4.8kV isolated sensors with differential sensing
customerCases.results: Passed 800V isolation requirements, improved accuracy to ±1%, achieved ASIL-C compliance

faqs.question: What isolation rating is required for 800V EV battery systems?
faqs.answer: For 800V EV battery systems, minimum 2.5kV working voltage isolation is recommended, with 4.8kV providing additional safety margin. According to IEC 60664, working voltage for basic insulation at 800V requires 1.6kV test voltage, while reinforced insulation requires 3.2kV. The ACS37610's 4.8kV rating exceeds both requirements. For compliance with UL 2580 (EV battery safety), the isolation must withstand 2kV + 2× working voltage (3.6kV for 800V systems). The 4.8kV rating provides margin for this requirement. Always verify specific safety certifications for your target markets and ensure compliance with local electrical safety regulations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 4.8kV isolation for 800V systems; ensures compliance with UL 2580 and IEC 60664; provides safety margin.

faqs.question: How fast does overcurrent protection need to respond?
faqs.answer: Overcurrent protection response time depends on the power devices being protected: SiC MOSFETs: 1-2μs withstand time during short circuit, require < 1μs protection response. IGBTs: 10μs withstand time, can tolerate 2-5μs response. Wiring and connectors: Can tolerate slower response (100μs to ms). The ACS37610 OCD responds in < 1μs, suitable for protecting SiC devices. System-level response includes: Sensor OCD (< 1μs) + Gate driver propagation (0.5-1μs) + MOSFET turn-off (0.5-2μs) = Total < 4μs. This is well within SiC device limits. For comparison, microcontroller-based protection typically takes 10-100μs, too slow for effective SiC protection. The OCD output can directly drive gate driver shutdown pins for fastest response.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use < 1μs protection for SiC devices; < 5μs for IGBTs; ACS37610 < 1μs OCD suitable for all applications.
